html:
Number 34

On their right as they entered, between the main door and the inner one, was the porterтАЩs lodge. An official of the French type, in the grey livery of the man at the station, was sitting at the telephone, reading the newspaper. He came out and led them through the well-lighted halls, on the left of which lay the reception-rooms. Hans Castorp peered in as he passed, but they were empty. Where, then, were the guests, he asked, and his cousin answered: тАЬIn the rest-cure. I had leave tonight to go out and meet you. Otherwise I am always up in my balcony, after supper.тАЭ

Hans Castorp came near bursting out again. тАЬWhat! You lie out on your balcony at night, in the damp?тАЭ he asked, his voice shaking.

тАЬYes, that is the rule. From eight to ten. But come and see your room now, and get a wash.тАЭ

They entered the liftтБатАФit was an electric one, worked by the Frenchman. As they went up, Hans Castorp wiped his eyes.

тАЬIтАЩm perfectly worn out with laughing,тАЭ he said, and breathed through his mouth. тАЬYouтАЩve told me such a lot of crazy stuffтБатАФthat about the psychoanalysis was the last straw. I suppose I am a bit relaxed from the journey. And my feet are coldтБатАФare yours? But my face burns so, it is really unpleasant. Do we eat now? I feel hungry. Is the food decent up here?тАЭ

They went noiselessly along the coco matting of the narrow corridor, which was lighted by electric lights in white glass shades set in the ceiling. The walls gleamed with hard white enamel paint. They had a glimpse of a nursing sister in a white cap, and eyeglasses on a cord that ran behind her ear. She had the look of a Protestant sisterтБатАФthat is to say, one working without a real vocation and burdened with restlessness and ennui. As they went along the corridor, Hans Castorp saw, beside two of the white-enamelled, numbered doors, certain curious, swollen-looking, balloon-shaped vessels with short necks. He did not think, at the moment, to ask what they were.

тАЬHere you are,тАЭ said Joachim. тАЬI am next you on the right. The other side you have a Russian couple, rather loud and offensive, but it couldnтАЩt be helped. Well, how do you like it?тАЭ

There were two doors, an outer and an inner, with clothes-hooks in the space between. Joachim had turned on the ceiling light, and in its vibrating brilliance the room looked restful and cheery, with practical white furniture, white washable walls, clean linoleum, and white linen curtains gaily embroidered in modern taste. The door stood open; one saw the lights of the valley and heard distant dance-music. The good Joachim had put a vase of flowers on the chest of drawersтБатАФa few bluebells and some yarrow, which he had found himself among the second crop of grass on the slopes.

тАЬAwfully decent of you,тАЭ said Hans Castorp. тАЬWhat a nice room! I can spend a couple of weeks here with pleasure.тАЭ

тАЬAn American woman died here day before yesterday,тАЭ said Joachim. тАЬBehrens told me directly that she would be out before you came, and you might have the room. Her fianc├й was with her, an English officer of marines, but he didnтАЩt behave very well. He kept coming out in the corridor to cry, just like a little boy. He rubbed cold cream on his cheeks, because he was close-shaven and the tears smarted. Night before last she had two first-class haemorrhages, and that was the finish. But she has been gone since yesterday morning, and after they took her away of course they fumigated the room thoroughly with formalin, which is the proper thing to use in such cases.тАЭ

Hans Castorp took in this information with a sprightly, yet half-distraught air. He was standing with his sleeves pushed back before the roomy wash-hand-basin, the taps of which shone in the electric light, and gave hardly a glance at the white metal bed with its fresh coverlet.

тАЬFumigated it, eh? ThatтАЩs ripping,тАЭ he said loquaciously and rather absurdly, as he washed and dried his hands. тАЬMethyl aldehyde; yes, thatтАЩs too much for the bacteria, no matter how strong they are. H2CO. But itтАЩs a powerful stench. Of course, perfect sanitation is absolutely essential.тАЭ He spoke with more of a Hamburg accent than his cousin, who had broken himself of it since his student days. Hans Castorp continued volubly. тАЬBut what I was about to say was, probably the officer of marines used a safety-razor; one makes oneself sore with those things easier than with a well-sharpened bladeтБатАФat least, that is my experience, and I use them both by turns. Well, and salt water would naturally make a tender skin smart, so he got in the way, in the service, of rubbing in cold cream. I donтАЩt see anything strange about thatтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ He rattled on: said that he had two hundred Maria Mancinis (his cigar) in his trunk, the customs officers had been very courteous; and gave his cousin greetings from various people at home. тАЬDonтАЩt they heat the rooms here?тАЭ he broke off to inquire, and ran to put his hands on the radiator.

тАЬNo, they keep us pretty cool,тАЭ answered Joachim. тАЬThe weather would have to be different from this before they put on the heat in August.тАЭ

тАЬAugust, August!тАЭ said Hans Castorp. тАЬBut I am cold, abominably cold; I mean in my body, for my face burns shockinglyтБатАФjust feel it!тАЭ

This demand was entirely foreign to the young manтАЩs natureтБатАФso much so that he himself was disagreeably impressed as he heard himself make it. Joachim did not take up the offer, but merely said: тАЬThat is the airтБатАФit doesnтАЩt mean anything; Behrens himself is purple in the face all day long. Some people never get used to it. Come along now, do, or we shanтАЩt get anything to eat.тАЭ

Outside they saw the nursing sister again, peering short-sightedly and inquisitively after them. But in the first storey Hans Castorp suddenly stopped, rooted to the spot by a perfectly ghastly sound coming from a little distance off round a bend in the corridor. It was not a loud sound, but so distinctly horrible that Hans Castorp made a wry face and looked wide-eyed at his cousin. It was coughing, obviously, a man coughing; but coughing like to no other Hans Castorp had ever heard, and compared with which any other had been a magnificent and healthy manifestation of life: a coughing that had no conviction and gave no relief, that did not even come out in paroxysms, but was just a feeble, dreadful welling up of the juices of organic dissolution.

тАЬYes,тАЭ said Joachim. тАЬThatтАЩs a bad case. An Austrian aristocrat, you know, very elegant. HeтАЩs a born horsemanтБатАФa gentleman rider. And now heтАЩs come to this. But he still gets about.тАЭ

As they went, Hans Castorp discoursed earnestly upon the gentleman riderтАЩs cough.

тАЬYou must realize,тАЭ he said, тАЬthat IтАЩve never heard anything like it before. It is entirely new to me, and naturally it makes a great impression. There are different kinds of cough, dry and loose, and people always say the loose one is better than the other, the barking kind. When I had croup, in my youthтАЭ (he actually said тАЬin my youthтАЭ!), тАЬI bayed like a wolf, and I can still remember how glad everybody was when it got looser. But a cough like thisтБатАФI didnтАЩt know there was such a cough! It isnтАЩt a human cough at all. It isnтАЩt dry and yet isnтАЩt loose eitherтБатАФthat is very far from being the right word for it. It is just as if one could look right into him when he coughs, and see what it looks like: all slime and mucousтБатАФтАЭ

тАЬOh,тАЭ said Joachim, тАЬI hear it every day, you donтАЩt need to describe it to me.тАЭ

But Hans Castorp could not get over the coughing he had heard. He kept repeating that he could see right into the gentleman riderтАЩs vitals; when they reached the restaurant his travel-weary eyes had an excited glitter.
